title: Indian Fairy Tales
description: Indian Fairy Tales — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "Indian Fairy Tales" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Table of Contents'

memes.label: "A Lac of Rupees for a Bit of Advice"
memes.description: "poor blind Brahman and his wife were dependent on their son for their subsistence. Every day the young fellow used to go out and get what he could by begging.…"

memes.label: "A Lesson for Kings"
memes.description: "nce upon a time, when Brahma-datta was reigning in Benares, the future Buddha returned to life as his son and heir. And when the day came for choosing a name,…"

memes.label: "Animal Helpers"
memes.description: "The motif of animals that aid human protagonists — dog, cat, snake, and other creatures who repay kindness with loyal service — a recurring pattern in Indian fairy tales reflecting the Buddhist value of compassion toward all beings"

memes.label: "Balna"
memes.description: "The clever youngest of seven princesses, imprisoned by the magician Punchkin, whose son's courage frees her and restores her petrified husband"

memes.label: "The Abduction of Balna"
memes.description: "The magician Punchkin, disguised as a holy beggar, forces his way into the palace and carries off the youngest queen Balna while her husband is away"

memes.label: "Benares"
memes.description: "The recurring holy royal city of northern India, seat of King Brahmadatta and the narrative backdrop for many of the collection's Jataka birth-stories"

memes.label: "Bengal"
memes.description: "The eastern region of the Indian subcontinent, cited as a documented source and comparative point for several tales in the collection's notes"

memes.label: "The Fables of Bidpai"
memes.description: "The ancient Indian collection of animal fables known as the Fables of Bidpai, a key transmission vector through which Indian narrative material traveled westward into Persian, Arabic, and European literature."

memes.label: "Bodhisatta (Bodhisattva)"
memes.description: "A being destined for enlightenment who, out of compassion, delays their own nirvana to help others — the central figure in the Jataka birth-stories that form the narrative framework of Indian fairy tales"

memes.label: "Buddha"
memes.description: "The enlightened teacher whose birth-stories (Jatakas) form the narrative frame of Indian fairy tales, appearing as the central moral figure across numerous tales."

memes.label: "Buddhist Moral Framework"
memes.description: "The Buddhist ethical framework that structures the moral lessons of the Jataka tales, using the Bodhisatta's virtuous actions to teach compassion, wisdom, and self-sacrifice"

relations.label: The Demon with the Matted Hair

memes.label: "Caste in Indian Folklore"
memes.description: "The social framework of caste that shapes the characters and conflicts of Indian fairy tales — where Brahmans, kings, merchants, and outcastes occupy distinct narrative roles reflecting historical Indian society"

memes.label: "Contents"
memes.description: "Table of contents for Indian Fairy Tales"

memes.label: "Death in Folklore"
memes.description: "The recurring theme of death in Indian folk tales, encompassing fatal tests, unjust executions, sacrificial deaths, and karmic consequences — a central narrative device in the Jataka and fairy tale traditions."

memes.label: "Demon"
memes.description: "Supernatural malevolent beings in Indian folklore — including Rakshasas and guardian demons — who menace heroes, guard treasures, or inhabit enchanted places."

memes.label: "Disguise and Deception"
memes.description: "The motif of disguise, shapeshifting, and deception — where characters transform their appearance or assume false identities to achieve their goals — a common narrative device in Indian fairy tales"

memes.label: "Faithfulness and Loyalty"
memes.description: "The steadfast devotion of true companions — above all loyal animals — set against human treachery, a moral that repeatedly rewards fidelity and exposes betrayal"

memes.label: "Family in Folklore"
memes.description: "The role of family bonds, kinship obligations, and intergenerational relationships in Indian fairy tales — family as both the source of a protagonist's identity and the domain of their deepest conflicts"

memes.label: "Fate and Prophecy"
memes.description: "The role of fate, prophecy, and predestination in Indian fairy tales — where cosmic forces and foretold destinies shape the narrative arc, reflecting the Buddhist and Hindu concept of karma unfolding across lives"

memes.label: "Fear in Folklore"
memes.description: "The motif of fear as a narrative driver in Indian fairy tales — fear of death, fear of kingship authority, and fear of supernatural forces propel characters into action and shape moral choices"

memes.label: Folly and Foolishness
memes.description: "The recurring motif of foolishness and its consequences in the Indian Fairy Tales — daydreaming, pride, loose talk, and poor judgment leading to ruin."

memes.label: Food and Feasting
memes.description: "The role of food, feasting, and eating in the Indian Fairy Tales — as reward, temptation, social ritual, and narrative turning point."

memes.label: "Gratitude and Ingratitude"
memes.description: "The recurring moral of repaying kindness with kindness — and the condemnation of those who betray a benefactor — one of the central ethical themes carried from the Jataka tales into Indian folk tradition"

memes.label: "Greed in Folklore"
memes.description: "The moral theme of greed leading to punishment — a recurring lesson in Indian fairy tales where excessive desire for wealth, power, or food results in the protagonist's downfall"

memes.label: Gulizar
memes.description: The beautiful princess of the Ivory City, whose name means "rosy-cheeked" in Persian, who falls in love with the prince and secretly betroths herself to him despite her father's opposition.

memes.label: "Harisarman"
memes.description: "here was a certain Brahman in a certain village, named Harisarman. He was poor and foolish and in evil case for want of employment, and he had very many…"

memes.label: "Harisarman"
memes.description: "A poor, foolish Brahman who feigns knowledge of magic and, carried along by sheer luck, wins fame and fortune as a false diviner"

memes.label: "Harisarman's Lucky Divination"
memes.description: "The impostor sage Harisarman survives three tests of his pretended magic by sheer chance — recovering a horse he hid himself, unmasking the thief Jihva by a slip of the tongue, and naming the frog in the pitcher through his childhood nickname"

memes.label: "Hiraman"
memes.description: "The wise parrot king who reveals Princess Labam's name and country to the Raja's son, setting the entire quest in motion"

memes.label: "How Sun, Moon, and Wind went out to Dinner"
memes.description: "ne day Sun, Moon, and Wind went out to dine with their uncle and aunts Thunder and Lightning. Their mother (one of the most distant Stars you see far up in the…"

memes.label: "How the Raja's Son won the Princess Labam"
memes.description: "a country there was a Raja who had an only son who every day went out to hunt. One day the Rani, his mother, said to him, 'You can hunt wherever you like on…"

memes.label: "How the Wicked Sons were Duped"
memes.description: "very wealthy old man, imagining that he was on the point of death, sent for his sons and divided his property among them. However, he did not die for several…"

memes.label: Husain Mahamat
memes.description: The Wazir's son and childhood companion of Prince Majnun in "Loving Laili," whose role transforms from loyal playmate to unintentional antagonist when he dismisses Majnun's vision of the simurgh and the fruit that releases Laili.

memes.label: "Full-page Illustrations"
memes.description: "[PRINCESS LABAM]() |  |  |  |  |  | _[Frontispiece]()_"

memes.label: "Ivory City"
memes.description: "The far-off marvellous city that is home to the fairy Princess Gulizar, the goal of the prince's quest in the tale of the ivory city"

memes.label: "Jataka Tales"
memes.description: "The Jataka tales are a vast collection of birth-stories of the Buddha, forming the narrative and moral framework for many Indian fairy tales collected by Joseph Jacobs"

memes.label: "Kashmir"
memes.description: "The northern Himalayan region whose folk-tale collection was a major documented source for several tales in the collection"

memes.label: "Katar the Horse"
memes.description: "The wicked, untameable fairy-horse from the fairies' country who swallows and protects the Moon Prince for two years, then carries him to freedom and magically disguises him"

memes.label: Khuda
memes.description: The divine being invoked across multiple tales in the "Indian Fairy Tales" collection, representing providence, fate, and the higher power that intervenes in human affairs.

memes.label: The King
memes.description: Recurring royal authority figure appearing across multiple Indian fairy tales as a ruler who sets trials, passes judgment, and gives daughters in marriage.

memes.label: King Brahmadatta
memes.description: The historical king of Benares during whose reign two Jataka birth-stories are set — "The Lion and the Crane" and "The Demon with the Matted Hair."

relations.label: The Demon with the Matted Hair

memes.label: King Dantal
memes.description: The king of the Phalana country and father of Prince Majnun in "Loving Laili," whose reign frames the tale's drama of love, loss, and restoration.

memes.label: King Mallika
memes.description: The named king of Kosala in "A Lesson for Kings," who meets King Brahma-datta of Benares on a narrow cart-track and becomes his mirror — an equally righteous ruler whose parallel virtue proves the tale's moral of universal humility.

memes.label: "Kingship and Rule"
memes.description: "The representation of kings and kingship in Indian fairy tales — the king as lawgiver, tester of worth, and moral exemplar whose authority structures the narrative while his fallibility drives dramatic tension"

memes.label: "Kokilan"
memes.description: "The newborn princess hidden in an underground palace by Raja Rasalu, who returns twelve years later to marry her when the mango tree flowers"

memes.label: "Loving Laili"
memes.description: "The devoted princess whose love for Prince Majnun survives death, ashes, and a series of magical transformations"

memes.label: "Lambikin"
memes.description: "The plump little lamb who talks his way past every hungry beast and rolls home hidden inside a drum, until his voice betrays him"

memes.label: "Lambikin's Drum Escape"
memes.description: "A plump lamb rolls home disguised inside a drum made of its brother's skin, escaping the beasts that mean to eat it"

memes.label: "Queen Lona"
memes.description: "The Queen of Raja Salabhan and mother of Raja Rasalu, who wept and prayed at many a shrine before being granted a son"

memes.label: "Love and Devotion"
memes.description: "The motif of love and devotion as a transformative and testing force in Indian fairy tales — romantic love drives quests, marital devotion survives supernatural trials, and love's endurance is the measure of a character's virtue"

memes.label: "Loving Laili"
memes.description: "nce there was a king called King Dantal, who had a great many rupees and soldiers and horses. He had also an only son called Prince Majnun, who was a handsome…"

memes.label: "Magical Objects"
memes.description: "Magical objects — rings, arrows, fruits, and other enchanted items — that drive the plot of Indian fairy tales, granting power, revealing truth, or bringing fortune to their possessors"

memes.label: Prince Majnun
memes.description: The devoted prince of the Phalana kingdom whose love for Laili transcends death, transformation, and separation in the tale "Loving Laili."

memes.label: "Marriage and Courtship"
memes.description: "The winning of a spouse — typically a prince who must complete impossible tasks or a princess who chooses her own husband — a driving engine of plot across the collection's romances"

memes.label: Moon
memes.description: A mythological personage, daughter of Star and sister of Sun and Wind, whose gentle remembrance of her mother earned her a blessing of cool, calm, and beautiful light.

memes.label: "Nature and Forest"
memes.description: "The forest as a liminal space in Indian fairy tales — a realm of danger and transformation, home to demons and tree-spirits, where protagonists are tested and civilization's rules are suspended"

memes.label: "Notes and References"
memes.description: "The story literature of India is in a large measure the outcome of the moral revolution of the peninsula connected with the name of Gautama Buddha. As the…"

memes.label: "Preface"
memes.description: "rom the extreme West of the Indo-European world, we go this year to the extreme East. From the soft rain and green turf of Gaeldom, we seek the garish sun and…"

memes.label: "Pride goeth before a Fall"
memes.description: "n a certain village there lived ten cloth merchants, who always went about together. Once upon a time they had travelled far afield, and were returning home…"

memes.label: The Prince
memes.description: The archetypal hero-prince who undertakes quests, wins princesses through trials, and appears as protagonist across multiple Indian fairy tales.

memes.label: "Princess Labam"
memes.description: "The radiant princess whose fame draws a prince across the world to win her through impossible tasks set by her father"

memes.label: "Punchkin"
memes.description: "nce upon a time there was a Raja who had seven beautiful daughters. They were all good girls; but the youngest, named Balna, was more clever than the rest. The…"

memes.label: "Punchkin (the Magician)"
memes.description: "The wicked magician whose life is hidden in a green parrot, who turns his victims to stone and imprisons Balna in a high tower"

memes.label: "The Death of Punchkin"
memes.description: "Balna's son destroys the green parrot that holds the magician Punchkin's life, killing him and freeing the princes he had turned to stone"

memes.label: "Raja Rasalu"
memes.description: "nce there lived a great Raja, whose name was Salabhan, and he had a Queen, by name Lona, who, though she wept and prayed at many a shrine, had never a child to…"

memes.label: "Raja Rasalu"
memes.description: "The hero-prince of Sialkot whose adventures culminate in a legendary game of chaupur against the tyrant King Sarkap"

memes.label: "Rasalu's Game of Chaupur with Sarkap"
memes.description: "The fatal game of chaupur in which Raja Rasalu, aided by a grateful kitten, defeats the tyrant-king Sarkap, wins his head, ends his tyranny, and gains the princess Kokilan"

memes.label: "Rebirth"
memes.description: "The Buddhist doctrine of rebirth that structures the Jataka tales, in which the Buddha and other figures are repeatedly reborn in different forms — animal, human, divine — across successive lifetimes governed by karma."

memes.label: "Reward and Punishment"
memes.description: "The moral principle in Indian fairy tales whereby characters receive their just deserts — kindness is rewarded, cruelty is punished, and ingratitude brings doom."

memes.label: "Riches and Wealth"
memes.description: "The motif of riches and wealth in Indian fairy tales — gold, treasure, and sudden prosperity as objects of desire, instruments of power, and moral tests that reveal character."

memes.label: "Royal Justice"
memes.description: "The ideal of the wise and righteous king whose just rule makes disputes vanish — and the testing of a ruler's or court's discernment through riddles and clever challenges"

memes.label: "Raja Salabhan"
memes.description: "The great Raja and father of Raja Rasalu, who, obeying a prophecy, hid his son underground for twelve years and never looked upon his face"

memes.label: "Gangazara"
memes.description: "The second son of a soothsayer who lives out his father's grim horoscope — poverty, imprisonment, and death by the sea — before rising to a throne"

memes.label: Sthuladatta
memes.description: A wealthy householder in "Harisarman" who employs the foolish Brahman Harisarman, setting the stage for the tale's satire of pretension and luck.

memes.label: "Sun"
memes.description: "The eldest child of Star, cursed with scorching heat for his selfishness in a tale explaining why the sun burns."

memes.label: "Supernatural Beings"
memes.description: "Supernatural beings — demons, ogres, spirits (Bongas), and other non-human entities — that populate Indian fairy tales as antagonists, guardians, and agents of transformation"

relations.label: The Demon with the Matted Hair

memes.label: "Suri the Cow"
memes.description: "The King's magical talking cow who swallows and protects the infant Moon Prince for two years, passing him to Katar the horse before her own death"

memes.label: "Talking Animals"
memes.description: "The motif of anthropomorphized animals that speak, reason, and act as moral agents — a central narrative device in Indian fairy tales and Jataka birth-stories"

memes.label: "Tests and Trials"
memes.description: "A recurring folklore motif in which characters must prove their worth, fidelity, or identity through tasks, tests, or ordeals set by kings, fakirs, or supernatural beings."

memes.label: The Ass
memes.description: A foolish ass who wears a lion's skin to deceive villagers but betrays himself by braying, leading to his death.

memes.label: "The Ass in the Lion's Skin"
memes.description: "t the same time, when Brahma-datta was reigning in Benares, the future Buddha was born one of a peasant family; and when he grew up, he gained his living by…"

memes.label: "The Bone in the Throat"
memes.description: "A white crane extracts a bone lodged in a lion's throat, only to be told that sparing his life is reward enough"
aliases: The Crane and the Lion's Bone

memes.label: The Bonga Girl
memes.description: A young woman drowned by her jealous sisters-in-law through a Bonga spirit's curse, transformed into a spirit and later into a magical bamboo fiddle, from which she emerges daily until discovered by the chief's son in "The Magic Fiddle."

memes.label: "The Boy who had a Moon on his Forehead and a Star on his Chin"
memes.description: "a country were seven daughters of poor parents, who used to come daily to play under the shady trees in the King's garden with the gardener's daughter; and…"

memes.label: "The Brahman"
memes.description: "A pious, soft-hearted Brahman whose mercy frees a trapped tiger and nearly costs him his life, until a clever jackal turns the tables"

memes.label: "Svabhavakripana"
memes.description: "The miserly Brahman whose elaborate fantasy of future wealth ends literally with a kick that shatters his pot of rice — and his dreams"
aliases: The Brahman of the Broken Pot

memes.label: The Brahmani
memes.description: The wife of a blind Brahman who, seeking her husband, receives three pieces of advice and sells them to a prince for a lac of rupees — becoming the hidden mechanism by which the prince survives three deadly trials, in "A Lac of Rupees for a Bit of Advice."

memes.label: "The Broken Pot"
memes.description: "here lived in a certain place a Brahman, whose name was Svabhavak _ri_ pa _n_ a, which means 'a born miser.' He had collected a quantity of rice by begging,…"

memes.label: "The Broken Pot Daydream"
memes.description: "A poor Brahman spins an elaborate fantasy of wealth and family over a single pot of rice, and destroys it in the act of imagining his own bad temper"

memes.label: The Cat
memes.description: A clever and resourceful cat, companion to the merchant's son, who recovers the charmed ring after it is stolen by an ogress.

memes.label: The Charioteer
memes.description: The charioteer of King Brahma-datta of Benares who, in "A Lesson for Kings," wins a roadside wisdom contest by proclaiming his king's true virtues — conquering anger by calmness, wickedness by goodness.

memes.label: "The Charmed Ring"
memes.description: "merchant started his son in life with three hundred rupees, and bade him go to another country and try his luck in trade. The son took the money and departed.…"

memes.label: The Chief's Son
memes.description: A young village chief who steals a magical fiddle from the Jogi, discovers the Bonga Girl emerging from it, and marries her, restoring her to human society in "The Magic Fiddle."

memes.label: The Crab
memes.description: A clever crab who sees through the deceitful crane's plan and turns the predator's own trick against him, killing the crane and saving himself.

memes.label: The Crane
memes.description: A compassionate crane who helps the Lion by removing a bone from his throat, only to be denied a reward — a Jataka tale character embodying the theme of ingratitude.

memes.label: The Crow
memes.description: A greedy crow who pretends friendship with a pigeon to access rich food, but whose gluttony leads to his disgrace and death.

memes.label: "The Cruel Crane"
memes.description: "The treacherous crane who lures a pond of trusting fish to their deaths, until a wary crab grips his neck and beheads him"

memes.label: "The Cruel Crane Outwitted"
memes.description: "ong ago the Bodisat was born to a forest life as the Genius of a tree standing near a certain lotus pond."

memes.label: "The Cruel Crane's Comeuppance"
memes.description: "A crab that the treacherous crane means to eat grips the bird's neck and beheads it, avenging the devoured fish"

memes.label: The Deccan
memes.description: "The Deccan plateau region of southern India, mentioned in the Notes and References as a source region for several tales in the collection."

memes.label: "The Demon with the Matted Hair"
memes.description: "_his story the Teacher told in Jetavana about a Brother who had ceased striving after righteousness. Said the Teacher to him: 'Is it really true that you have…"

memes.label: "The Demon's Capture"
memes.description: "The encounter in Chapter 25 where the Bodhisatta Prince of the Five Weapons is caught by the Demon with the Matted Hair, escapes through fearlessness, and converts the Demon to righteousness."

relations.label: The Demon with the Matted Hair

memes.label: The Dog
memes.description: A loyal but jealous dog, companion to the merchant's son, who helps recover the charmed ring but twice carelessly drops it.

memes.label: The Elephant
memes.description: A grand animal appearing across multiple tales as a royal mount and symbol of kingly wealth and power, frequently given as a prestigious gift.

memes.label: "The External Soul (Life-Index)"
memes.description: "The motif in which a villain's life is hidden outside the body in an external object or creature, so that the hero can slay the seemingly invincible enemy only by destroying that hidden life"

memes.label: The Fakir
memes.description: "A mysterious Fakir who bargains with a childless King for one of his future sons in the tale \"The Prince and the Fakir.\""

relations.label: The Prince's Test by the Fakir

memes.label: The Farmer
memes.description: A recurring commoner figure in Indian fairy tales, appearing as both a struggling cultivator who receives supernatural aid and a clever peasant who outwits his oppressor.

memes.label: "The Farmer and the Money-lender"
memes.description: "here was once a farmer who suffered much at the hands of a money-lender. Good harvests, or bad, the farmer was always poor, the money-lender rich. At the last,…"

memes.label: "The Farmer's Daughter"
memes.description: "The unnamed but sharp-witted daughter of an old farmer who alone can interpret the vizier's son's riddles and solves the mystery of the laughing fish in Why the Fish Laughed"

memes.label: The Fish
memes.description: Fish characters appearing across multiple Indian fairy tales as victims of trickery and as bearers of unexpected wisdom, most notably in "The Cruel Crane Outwitted" and "Why the Fish Laughed."

memes.label: "The Fish's Laughter and the Riddle Solved"
memes.description: "The dead fish that laughs at the queen exposes a man disguised among her maids; the vizier's son, guided by a farmer's clever daughter, reads the riddle and saves his father from execution"

memes.label: "The Forest"
memes.description: "The wooded wilderness of the tales — a place of tree-spirits and ascetic 'forest life', of dense woods travellers ride through, and of enchanted or demon-haunted groves the hero must pass to reach his goal"

memes.label: "The Garden"
memes.description: "The walled royal pleasure-garden attached to the palace — a lush enclosure of fruit trees and flowers where lovers meet, princesses hide, magic transformations occur, and secret trysts unfold"

memes.label: The Gardener
memes.description: A gardener in the king's service whose daughter secretly marries a prince with a moon on his forehead and a star on his chin.

memes.label: The Gardener's Daughter
memes.description: A poor gardener's daughter who prophesies she will bear a son with a moon on his forehead and a star on his chin; married by the King, she endures the persecution of four jealous Queens before her prophecy is fulfilled.

memes.label: "The Gold-giving Serpent"
memes.description: "ow in a certain place there lived a Brahman named Haridatta. He was a farmer, but poor was the return his labour brought him. One day, at the end of the hot…"

memes.label: "The Gold-giving Serpent"
memes.description: "The hooded serpent-deity of a Brahman's field that rewards daily worship with a gold coin, until human greed breaks the bond forever"

memes.label: "The Golden Serpent's Broken Bond"
memes.description: "The Brahman's son strikes at the gold-giving serpent to seize its whole treasure, and dies of its bite — shattering forever the daily bond of gold between the serpent and the Brahman's family"
aliases: The Breaking of the Serpent's Friendship
The Brahman's Son and the Serpent

memes.label: The Goldsmith
memes.description: A goldsmith named Manikkasari whom Gangazara rescues from a well, only to be betrayed by him in a false accusation of murder — embodying the folk warning that goldsmiths are never to be trusted.

memes.label: "The Granny"
memes.description: "The indulgent grandmother who fattens Lambikin in the corn-bin and builds him the drumikin that conceals him on his fateful journey home"

memes.label: The Hawker
memes.description: An itinerant trader in the Jataka fable "The Ass in the Lion's Skin," who disguises his ass in a lion's skin to let it graze in forbidden fields, only to see his deception exposed when the ass brays.

relations.label: The Ass in the Lion's Skin

memes.label: "The Himalayas"
memes.description: "The great snow-clad northern mountain range that serves in the Jataka-derived tales as a remote, pristine wilderness — the birth-region of the Bodhisatta-beasts and the setting for animal fables far from human society"

memes.label: The Hunter
memes.description: One of the king's hunters who reveals to the soothsayer's son the truth about his father's murder, in "The Soothsayer's Son."

memes.label: "The Ivory City and its Fairy Princess"
memes.description: "ne day a young prince was out practising archery with the son of his father's chief vizier, when one of the arrows accidentally struck the wife of a merchant,…"

memes.label: "The Jackal"
memes.description: "The quick-witted jackal who feigns confusion to trick an ungrateful tiger back into its cage and save the Brahman"

memes.label: "The Jackal's Cage Trick"
memes.description: "A jackal feigns bewilderment to coax an ungrateful tiger back into the cage from which a Brahman had freed it"
aliases: The Tiger Back in the Cage

memes.label: The Jogi
memes.description: A wandering Jogi (ascetic) who discovers an enchanted bamboo and fashions it into a magical fiddle in "The Magic Fiddle"; Jogis appear across several tales as wandering holy men who own magical animals and grant boons.
aliases: The Jogi of the Magic Fiddle

memes.label: "The Jungle"
memes.description: "The dense, trackless wild that forms the default landscape of the collection's animal and quest tales — a lawless domain of tigers, jackals and fakirs where heroes lose their way, princesses are abandoned, and beasts hold power"

memes.label: "The Lambikin"
memes.description: "nce upon a time there was a wee wee Lambikin, who frolicked about on his little tottery legs, and enjoyed himself amazingly."

memes.label: "The Lion"
memes.description: "The ungrateful lion in the Jataka tale who refuses to reward the crane that saved his life."
aliases: the Lion in the Lion and the Crane

memes.label: "The Lion and the Crane"
memes.description: "he Bodhisatta was at one time born in the region of Himavanta as a white crane; now Brahmadatta was at that time reigning in Benares. Now it chanced that as a…"

memes.label: "The Magic Fiddle"
memes.description: "nce upon a time there lived seven brothers and a sister. The brothers were married, but their wives did not do the cooking for the family. It was done by their…"

memes.label: "The Magic Fiddle Resurrection"
memes.description: "A girl drowned by her jealous sisters-in-law is reborn through a chain of transformations — spirit, bamboo, and fiddle — and finally restored to human form before her own family"

memes.label: The Maid
memes.description: Female attendants and maidens appearing across multiple Indian fairy tales, most notably as Jihva the named maid in "Harisarman," the handmaiden in "The Prince and the Fakir," and the seventy maidens of King Sarkap in "Raja Rasalu."

memes.label: The Demon with the Matted Hair
memes.description: "A fearsome man-eating Demon who inhabits a forest on the road to Benares and is subdued by the Bodhisatta Prince of the Five Weapons in a Jataka tale."

memes.label: The Merchant
memes.description: A merchant character appearing in multiple tales, most notably as the aggrieved husband whose wife is struck by the prince's stray arrow in "The Ivory City."

memes.label: "The Money-lender"
memes.description: "A grasping village usurer whose insatiable greed for double whatever his victim gains finally lures him to a self-made death"

memes.label: "The Moon Prince"
memes.description: "The prophesied child born with a moon on his forehead and a star on his chin, stolen at birth by jealous Queens, raised by animal guardians, and destined to win a princess through his singing"

memes.label: The Nurse
memes.description: A recurring background figure in the tales, appearing as caretakers of royal children — including the nurse tasked with killing the moon-forehead boy who instead abandons him.

memes.label: The Old Farmer
memes.description: A simple farmer who hosts a mysterious young traveller (the vizier's son in disguise) and whose daughter eventually marries him, in "Why the Fish Laughed."
aliases: The Farmer of Why the Fish Laughed

memes.label: The Old Woman
memes.description: A kind old woman who shelters the Raja's son in the Princess Labam's country despite the king's prohibition against harbouring foreigners, and becomes his indispensable ally in winning the princess.
aliases: The Old Woman of Labam's Country

memes.label: "The Palace"
memes.description: "The royal residence and seat of power of rajas, ranis, and their courts — a recurring built setting across the tales where princes are raised, princesses are guarded, thieves strike, and fortunes are won"

memes.label: "The Parrot"
memes.description: "The little green parrot that holds the magician Punchkin's life; when it is destroyed, the magician dies"

memes.label: "The Pigeon"
memes.description: "A wise pigeon, an incarnation of the Bodhisatta, who befriends a greedy crow in a Jataka fable."

memes.label: "The Pigeon and the Crow"
memes.description: "nce upon a time the Bodhisatta was a Pigeon, and lived in a nest-basket which a rich man's cook had hung up in the kitchen, in order to earn merit by it. A…"

memes.label: "The Potter"
memes.description: "A potter who laughs and cries over his son's fate, and unwittingly helps the disguised prince slay the snake-demons."

memes.label: The Prince
memes.description: A recurring royal youth archetype appearing across multiple Indian fairy tales, embodying the hero's journey through quest, exile, courtship, and triumph.

memes.label: "The Prince and the Fakir"
memes.description: "here was once upon a time a King who had no children. Now this King went and laid him down to rest at a place where four roads met, so that every one who…"

memes.label: "The Prince of the Five Weapons"
memes.description: "The Bodhisatta born as Prince of Benares, armed with the five weapons and the weapon of knowledge within, who faces the Demon with the Matted Hair without fear"

relations.label: The Demon with the Matted Hair

memes.label: "The Prince's Test by the Fakir"
memes.description: "The fatal confrontation between the Prince and the Fakir in Chapter 23: the Fakir attempts to boil the Prince alive in a caldron of oil, but the Prince throws the Fakir in instead."

memes.label: The Princess
memes.description: A recurring royal maiden archetype appearing across multiple Indian fairy tales, ranging from the divinely destined Laili to the clever Princess Labam who secretly aids her suitor through deadly trials.

memes.label: The Queen
memes.description: A recurring royal female figure appearing across multiple Indian fairy tales, including as the queen who loses her pearl rosary and the queen who inquires about a laughing fish.

memes.label: The Rat
memes.description: The rat community whose prince's wedding is disrupted by the Cat, forcing them to recover the charmed ring from the ogress.

memes.label: "The River"
memes.description: "The flowing river that heroes must cross, bathe in, or ford on their journeys — a boundary and ritual site where talking fish live, disguises are made ready, and the ordinary world is left behind"

memes.label: The Road
memes.description: "The Road as a narrative setting in the Indian Fairy Tales — a liminal space of encounter, danger, and transformation."

relations.label: The Demon with the Matted Hair

memes.label: The Robber
memes.description: Criminal figures appearing in several tales, most notably as three uneducated robbers outwitted by cloth merchants in "Pride Goeth Before a Fall."

memes.label: "The Sea"
memes.description: "The sea and its shore, which appear in the collection chiefly as the appointed place of a prophesied death and as the measure in a riddle — the edge of the known world where fate overtakes the traveller rather than a route for voyages"

memes.label: The Sepoy
memes.description: The native Indian soldier-guards who serve the king, appearing as armed retainers in "The Boy Who Had a Moon on His Forehead and a Star on His Chin."

memes.label: The Servant
memes.description: A recurring figure in Indian fairy tales, appearing as a faithful attendant, a disguised hero, or a witness to key events across multiple tales including "The Boy Who Had a Moon on His Forehead" and "The Soothsayer's Son."

memes.label: The Slave
memes.description: Enslaved figures appearing across multiple Indian fairy tales, serving as messengers, attendants, and narrative devices that illuminate the power structures of royal courts.

memes.label: The Snake
memes.description: The serpent prince, son of Raja Indrasha, who is saved by the merchant's son and rewards him with magical treasures.

memes.label: The Son of Seven Mothers
memes.description: The unnamed hero of "The Son of Seven Queens," born in prison to seven blinded queens, who escapes to feed his mothers, outwits a demon Jogi king, and kills the treacherous white hind to restore his mothers' sight.
aliases: The Boy Born of Seven Queens

memes.label: "The Son of Seven Queens"
memes.description: "nce upon a time there lived a King who had seven Queens, but no children. This was a great grief to him, especially when he remembered that on his death there…"

memes.label: "The Soothsayer's Son"
memes.description: "soothsayer when on his deathbed wrote out the horoscope of his second son, whose name was Gangazara, and bequeathed it to him as his only property, leaving the…"

memes.label: "The Soothsayer's Inherited Verse"
memes.description: "The single prophetic horoscope a dying soothsayer bequeaths to his second son — foretelling poverty, imprisonment, death on the sea-shore, and happiness after — whose every line comes true and whose faith carries the son through the desert and the fatal well"

memes.label: The Step-Mother
memes.description: The cruel second wife of the Raja in "Punchkin" who starves her seven step-daughters, destroys their mother's grave-gifts, and ultimately schemes to have them killed.

memes.label: "The Talkative Tortoise"
memes.description: "he future Buddha was once born in a minister's family, when Brahma-datta was reigning in Benares; and when he grew up, he became the king's adviser in things…"

memes.label: The Teacher
memes.description: A figure of learning and instruction appearing across the Indian Fairy Tales, from the tutor of princes to the far-famed teacher of Takkasila in a Jataka-derived tale.

relations.label: The Demon with the Matted Hair

memes.label: "The Temple"
memes.description: "The temple or shrine — a place of worship, offering, and miracle in the tales, where devotees pray for children, gods are called upon to grant boons, and charity is dispensed in the king's name"

memes.label: The Thief
memes.description: A recurring antagonist figure in the Indian Fairy Tales, appearing as both a solitary criminal and as part of bands of thieves who test the wisdom of kings and the cleverness of protagonists.

memes.label: "The Tiger"
memes.description: "The ungrateful tiger who, freed from its cage by a kindly Brahman, at once resolves to devour its rescuer"

memes.label: "The Tiger, the Brahman, and the Jackal"
memes.description: "nce upon a time, a tiger was caught in a trap. He tried in vain to get out through the bars, and rolled and bit with rage and grief when he failed."

memes.label: "The Tortoise"
memes.description: "A tortoise who could not hold his tongue and fell to his death, becoming a lesson for a talkative king."

memes.label: "The Tortoise's Fatal Fall"
memes.description: "A tortoise borne aloft on a stick by two wild ducks opens its mouth to retort and plunges to its death"

memes.label: The Traveller
memes.description: A recurring figure in Indian fairy tales who journeys across kingdoms — sometimes a prince in disguise, sometimes a genuine wanderer — appearing in "The Prince and the Fakir," "Why the Fish Laughed," and "The Ivory City."

memes.label: "The Village"
memes.description: "The rural settlement where Brahmans, farmers, merchants, and other commoners live — the everyday world of the tales, headed by a village chief and set against the courtly world of the palace"

memes.label: The Vizier
memes.description: A high-ranking royal advisor appearing in "Why the Fish Laughed" and "The Ivory City and Its Fairy Princess," representing wisdom and loyalty in the face of royal authority.

memes.label: "The Vizier's Son"
memes.description: "The unnamed but resourceful companion of the exiled prince in The Ivory City and its Fairy Princess, whose cleverness drives every plot advance, and who also appears as a riddle-solving traveler in Why the Fish Laughed"

memes.label: "The Well"
memes.description: "The well as place — a deep shaft in the countryside that recurs as a plot device where creatures fall in and are trapped, prisoners are lowered, and irrigation water is drawn by the well-wheel"

memes.label: The White Hind
memes.description: A magical white hind with golden horns and silver hoofs who transforms into a beautiful maiden, bewitches the King, and cruelly usurps the seven Queens in "The Son of Seven Queens."

memes.label: The Wild Ducks
memes.description: Two hamsas (wild ducks) who befriend a tortoise and attempt to carry him to the Himalayas by stick, only for the tortoise's talkativeness to undo their plan in the Jataka fable "The Talkative Tortoise."

memes.label: "Trickster Figure"
memes.description: "The trickster archetype — a clever, cunning character who uses intelligence and deception to outwit stronger opponents — a recurring figure in Indian fairy tales, most commonly embodied by the jackal"

memes.label: "Ujjain"
memes.description: "An ancient royal city of central India, twenty kas south of the main road to Benares, ruled by its own king in the tale of the soothsayer's son"

memes.label: "Why the Fish Laughed"
memes.description: "s a certain fisherwoman passed by a palace crying her fish, the queen appeared at one of the windows and beckoned her to come near and show what she had. At…"

memes.label: Wind
memes.description: A mythological personage, son of Star and brother of Sun and Moon, whose selfish forgetfulness of his mother earned him a curse of parching, destructive heat.

memes.label: "The Winning of Princess Labam"
memes.description: "A Raja's son learns of the hidden Princess Labam, journeys to her distant country with magic gifts, and wins her hand by surviving her father's deadly trials"

memes.label: "Wisdom and Cleverness"
memes.description: "The recurring moral motif in Indian fairy tales where intelligence, quick thinking, and verbal skill overcome brute force, wealth, or social power."

memes.label: "Worship and Devotion"
memes.description: "The practice of worship and religious devotion in Indian fairy tales, encompassing Hindu, Buddhist, and Islamic traditions — from offerings to Ganesa to prayers at saints' tombs."
